ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Fu Yong Egg. The closest parent is the generic omelette food product class (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Fu Yong Egg. A00LC (Hen eggs, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Fu Yong Egg or a sufficiently close egg-based dish concept.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bean sprouts, spring onions, carrots, peas)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Fu Yong Egg is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a single large pan-fried egg omelette with vegetables on a plate.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, scrambled / omelette with vegetables — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet (NDB 01128) and vegetable additions estimated from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Fu Yong Egg is a pan-fried egg omelette with mixed vegetables. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo egg-based dish entries and USDA SR Legacy NDB 01128 (Egg, whole, cooked, omelet) with vegetable additions (bean sprouts, spring onions, carrots) blended at approximately 70% egg mass and 30% vegetable mass, plus cooking oil contribution. Values represent a composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and omelette analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fu Yong Egg. GI is estimated at 32 (low) based on the analogous food 'omelette / egg-based dish' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Egg-based dishes are consistently low-GI due to negligible starch content. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 3.2 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 2.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving contributes approximately 620 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg per day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 32) and low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) result in minimal postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fu Yong Egg is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, low-GI dish; pairing with non-starchy vegetables further supports glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content approximately 280 mg per 100 g from egg yolks; saturated fat 2.8 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption to one serving per occasion and choose cooking methods that minimise added oil to limit saturated fat and dietary cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fu Yong Egg (芙蓉蛋, also romanised as Fu Rong Dan) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean egg dish consisting of beaten whole eggs pan-fried or lightly stir-fried with a mixture of vegetables such as bean sprouts, spring onions, carrots, and green peas. The result is a thick, fluffy omelette with a golden-brown exterior and a soft, moist interior. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, salt, and white pepper, and is sometimes finished with a light savoury gravy poured over the top. Fu Yong Egg is a staple in Singapore coffee shops (kopitiams), Chinese zichar stalls, and casual restaurants, where it is typically served as a shared side dish or as a main with steamed rice. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycaemic index (estimated GI 32), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The egg base provides high-quality complete protein (approximately 9.2 g per 100 g), essential B vitamins including B12, riboflavin, and folate, as well as vitamin D and selenium. The sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free in its traditional form but may contain soy allergens from soy sauce. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Fu Yong Egg?
Is Fu Yong Egg good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Fu Yong Egg is not listed as a discrete entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central under this exact name. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01128 (cooked whole egg omelette) with vegetable additions estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database egg-based dish category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01128 (Egg, whole, cooked, omelet) at approximately 70% egg mass and 30% mixed vegetable mass, with cooking oil contribution included. No single approved-source entry for Fu Yong Egg as a named dish was found; values are adapted. 2. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on egg-based omelette dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Fu Yong Egg was found. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked egg omelette and mixed vegetables; no sentinel -1 values are used as reasonable estimates are available from approved sources. 4. The input cuisine tag 'Filipino' has been corrected to 'Chinese-Singaporean' as Fu Yong Egg is a Chinese-origin dish widely served in Singapore; the Filipino attribution in the field survey seed record likely reflects the restaurant's broader menu category rather than the dish's culinary origin. 5. Halal status is conditionally true assuming no pork-derived ingredients are used in preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 3.2 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 2.0 g for a 200 g serving.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Filipino' to 'Chinese-Singaporean' based on culinary origin evidence.
Added all four mandatory non-English local names for Singaporean dish category: Chinese (芙蓉蛋), Malay (Telur Fu Yong), Tamil (ஃபு யோங் முட்டை), Hindi (फू योंग अंडा).
Derived nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01128 adapted with vegetable additions per approved source policy.
Assigned GI of 32 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg-based dish category.
Selected FoodOn closest_parent FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 closest_parent A00LC as defensible ontology anchors without inventing codes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Documented all adapted and estimated values in notes and nutrient_source fields.
Confirmed no sentinel -1 values are used as all nutrient fields have defensible estimates from approved sources.
